content:
The debate keeps happening in the abstract. Here’s what happens when you get specific!

7 min read
Just now

--

Every few months, the same headline resurfaces in a slightly different costume: AI is coming for Data Engineering jobs!!
Sometimes it’s framed as a warning, sometimes as a promise and almost always as a single yes-or-no question: will AI replace Data Engineers or won’t it?
That question is the wrong shape. “Data Engineer” isn’t one task, it’s a bundle of a dozen very different tasks and AI’s ability to do them ranges from “already better than most humans” to “not close, and not obviously getting there soon.”
Arguing about the job as a whole just means two people talking past each other, one thinking about SQL generation and the other thinking about incident response at 2:39 a.m.
So I did the boring, useful thing instead: broke the job into its actual pieces and rated each one honestly. Some of what I found will be reassuring.
Some of it won’t be. Recent industry surveys put daily AI usage among Data Engineers above 80% at this point, the debate about whether to use it is basically over. The real, more uncomfortable question is which parts of the job that usage is actually replacing and which parts it’s just making faster.
